title: Listening - Part 4
description: For each question, choose the correct answer.
instructions: You will hear an interview. For questions 20–25, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear.
context: You will hear an interview with a boy called Ethan Marsh, who plays chess in national competitions.
audio_script: Interviewer: Today we're speaking to Ethan Marsh, a young chess player who has already won several national competitions. Ethan, how did you first get interested in chess? || Guest: My grandfather had a chessboard in his living room, but I only watched him play at first. Then one rainy holiday, he showed me the moves. [20]I liked that every game felt like a puzzle I could solve in a different way[/20], so I kept asking to play. || Interviewer: Did you start entering competitions straight away? || Guest: No, I was nervous at first. My first club competition was in a school hall, and I lost the first two games. [21]What bothered me most was sitting silently for so long while everyone else looked completely calm[/21]. I wasn't used to that. || Interviewer: How do you prepare before an important match? || Guest: Some players memorise long lists of openings. I do a little of that, but [22]I spend more time looking back at my old games to see the mistakes I keep making[/22]. It helps me avoid repeating them. || Interviewer: What happened when you won your first national title? || Guest: I was pleased, of course, but it felt strange. [23]I expected to feel confident afterwards, but actually I worried people would expect me to win every match[/23]. That pressure took time to handle. || Interviewer: Do your friends understand your interest in chess? || Guest: Some do. Others think it's just a quiet board game. [24]They don't realise how tiring it is to concentrate hard for several hours[/24]. After a tournament I feel as tired as I do after sport. || Interviewer: What would you like to do next? || Guest: I'm working with a coach online, which is useful. But [25]I'd really like to play in a tournament abroad, because meeting players from other countries would teach me a lot[/25]. It would also be exciting to travel because of chess. || Interviewer: Do you think chess helps you at school? || Guest: Yes, but not in a simple way. It doesn't make homework easy, but it helps me stay patient. If a maths problem looks difficult, I don't panic as quickly. I try one method, then another, like in a game. Teachers say I think more carefully now. || Interviewer: Do you ever get bored with it? || Guest: Sometimes, especially when I study alone for too long. Then I take a break and play football or go cycling. Chess is important to me, but I don't want it to become the only thing I do. Having other interests makes me play better, I think. || Interviewer: How do you deal with losing an important game? || Guest: I used to feel awful for days, but my coach helped me see a lost game as information. After a match we look at one or two key moves, not every mistake. That makes it less painful and much more useful. || Interviewer: Do you prefer playing online or face to face? || Guest: Online chess is convenient, especially on school nights, but face-to-face games feel more serious. You notice the other player thinking, and you have to control your own expression. That is part of the challenge, and I think it makes me stronger.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Today we're speaking to Ethan Marsh, a young chess player who has already won several national competitions. Ethan, how did you first get interested in chess?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: My grandfather had a chessboard in his living room, but I only watched him play at first. Then one rainy holiday, he showed me the moves. I liked that every game felt like a puzzle I could solve in a different way, so I kept asking to play.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Did you start entering competitions straight away?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: No, I was nervous at first. My first club competition was in a school hall, and I lost the first two games. What bothered me most was sitting silently for so long while everyone else looked completely calm. I wasn't used to that.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: How do you prepare before an important match?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Some players memorise long lists of openings. I do a little of that, but I spend more time looking back at my old games to see the mistakes I keep making. It helps me avoid repeating them.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What happened when you won your first national title?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I was pleased, of course, but it felt strange. I expected to feel confident afterwards, but actually I worried people would expect me to win every match. That pressure took time to handle.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do your friends understand your interest in chess?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Some do. Others think it's just a quiet board game. They don't realise how tiring it is to concentrate hard for several hours. After a tournament I feel as tired as I do after sport.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What would you like to do next?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I'm working with a coach online, which is useful. But I'd really like to play in a tournament abroad, because meeting players from other countries would teach me a lot. It would also be exciting to travel because of chess.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do you think chess helps you at school?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Yes, but not in a simple way. It doesn't make homework easy, but it helps me stay patient. If a maths problem looks difficult, I don't panic as quickly. I try one method, then another, like in a game. Teachers say I think more carefully now.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do you ever get bored with it?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Sometimes, especially when I study alone for too long. Then I take a break and play football or go cycling. Chess is important to me, but I don't want it to become the only thing I do. Having other interests makes me play better, I think.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: How do you deal with losing an important game?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I used to feel awful for days, but my coach helped me see a lost game as information. After a match we look at one or two key moves, not every mistake. That makes it less painful and much more useful.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do you prefer playing online or face to face?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Online chess is convenient, especially on school nights, but face-to-face games feel more serious. You notice the other player thinking, and you have to control your own expression. That is part of the challenge, and I think it makes me stronger.

questions.question: Why did Ethan first enjoy chess?

options.A: He wanted to copy his grandfather.
options.B: He enjoyed solving problems in different ways.

questions.explanation: Ethan says every game felt like a puzzle he could solve in a different way.

questions.question: What did Ethan find difficult at his first competition?

options.C: Keeping quiet and still for a long time.

questions.explanation: He says sitting silently for so long bothered him most.

questions.question: How does Ethan usually prepare for matches?

options.A: He studies his own previous games.
options.B: He memorises as many openings as possible.
options.C: He practises with friends from school.

questions.explanation: He says he spends more time looking back at his old games to see his mistakes.

questions.question: How did Ethan feel after winning his first national title?
questions.explanation: He says he worried people would expect him to win every match.

questions.question: What do some of Ethan’s friends not understand about chess?

options.B: How many rules players must learn.
options.C: How tiring it is to concentrate for hours.

questions.explanation: He says they do not realise how tiring it is to concentrate hard for several hours.

questions.question: What would Ethan like to do next?
questions.explanation: He says he would like to play in a tournament abroad.
